planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, very acidic, sandy, low-nutrient soil in full sun to partial shade; dull meadow beauty grows naturally in wet longleaf pine savannas, flatwoods seeps, and pitcher plant bogs of the southeastern Coastal Plain. It requires acidic, nutrient-poor, moist sandy conditions to thrive.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), sandy, low-nutrient soil in full sun to partial shade; dull meadow beauty is a taller, more robust native Rhexia of the southeastern longleaf pine savanna ecosystem, producing clusters of pale rose-pink to nearly white four-petaled flowers with the distinctive curved yellow anthers typical of all Rhexia species. It grows in a narrower ecological range than Maryland meadow beauty, primarily in the wet flatwoods and pitcher plant bogs of the lower Coastal Plain from North Carolina to Louisiana. Like all meadow beauties, it is pollinated primarily by buzz-pollinating bumblebees that vibrate the anthers at the precise frequency to release pollen. The characteristic jug-shaped, urn-necked seed capsule is ornamental in autumn. Requires the exact conditions of its longleaf pine savanna habitat — very acidic, moist, sandy, and nutrient-poor — making it a specialist plant for bog gardens and wet pine savanna restorations.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall near established oaks in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean, open woodland soil in partial shade; large yellow false foxglove is hemiparasitic on oak roots and cannot survive without an established oak host. Plant near black oak, bur oak, or white oak.

directions: Direct sow in fall near established oaks in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean woodland soil in partial shade; large yellow false foxglove is one of the most striking native hemiparasites of the eastern US oak woodlands, producing large, brilliant yellow, two-lipped tubular flowers — the largest flowers in the genus Aureolaria — on upright stems through late summer. Like all Aureolaria species, it taps the root systems of nearby oaks for supplemental water and nutrients while also producing chlorophyll; it cannot survive without an oak host plant. The deeply cut, almost fern-like foliage is attractive throughout the season. The large, bright yellow flowers are strongly attractive to bumblebees, hummingbirds, and clearwing sphinx moths in late summer when few other native woodland plants are in bloom. A rewarding and ecologically important native for naturalistic oak woodland gardens, particularly in the central US.

planting.note: Direct sow near established oaks in spring in well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic, lean, open or rocky woodland soil in partial shade to full sun; fernleaf false foxglove is an annual hemiparasite that self-sows near oak roots in open, dry oak barrens, pine-oak woods, and acidic shale barrens.

directions: Direct sow near established oaks in spring in well-drained, dry to average, slightly acidic, lean soil in partial shade to full sun; fernleaf false foxglove is an annual or short-lived perennial hemiparasite of dry oak barrens, pine-oak woodlands, and acidic shale barrens of the eastern US, producing deeply pinnately dissected, fern-like foliage (giving the plant its common name) and clusters of bright yellow, tubular two-lipped flowers in summer. The extremely finely cut leaves are among the most distinctive and attractive of any Aureolaria species, making the plant recognizable even without flowers. Like all Aureolaria, it taps the roots of nearby oaks for supplemental water and nutrients; unlike the perennial species, it completes its cycle in a single season and perpetuates by self-sowing. More tolerant of dry, sunny, open conditions than the perennial Aureolaria species. A specialist plant of high ecological value in oak barrens conservation and restoration.

planting.note: Plant fresh rhizome divisions or fresh seeds in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in shade; giant trillium requires two cold stratification periods (double dormancy) to germinate from seed and is slowest grown from division. Plant in deep shade with consistent organic matter.

directions: Plant rhizomes in fall in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deeply organic woodland soil in deep shade under large-canopy trees; giant trillium is the western counterpart of the eastern large trilliums, native to moist redwood forests, mixed evergreen forests, and oak woodlands of the California Coast Ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills. The large, mottled, dark-marbled leaves — held in the characteristic trillium pattern of three at a node — are among the most distinctive spring ephemeral foliage in western North America, and the upright, sessile flowers range from white through deep red-maroon, with the maroon or dark red forms especially striking. The flowers are carrion-scented, attracting fly pollinators rather than bees. Like eastern trilliums, seeds require two years of stratification and plants require 5–7 years to reach flowering from seed. One of the most spectacular native wildflowers for deep woodland shade gardens in California and the Pacific Northwest.

common_name: Western White Fawn Lily (Oregon Fawn Lily)

planting.note: Plant corms in fall (2–3 inches deep) in well-drained to dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or loamy woodland soil in partial shade to shade; western white fawn lily grows naturally in open, rocky oak and Douglas fir woodlands and grassy balds of the Pacific Northwest. Goes completely summer-dormant after setting seed.

directions: Plant corms 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained to dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or loamy woodland soil in partial shade; western white fawn lily is a native spring ephemeral of the Pacific Northwest, producing pairs of beautiful, mottled, brown-and-green basal leaves and nodding, creamy-white to white flowers with reflexed petals that reveal bright yellow anthers within — an elegant, understated woodland lily of great beauty. After the seeds ripen in late spring, the plant retreats completely underground until the following spring — perfect under deciduous trees where summer shade would harm other plants. Native to open Garry oak woodlands, rocky slopes, and the margins of Douglas fir forests from Vancouver Island south through Oregon. The mottled leaves (reminiscent of a fawn's spotted coat, giving the genus its common name) are almost as ornamental as the flowers. Multiply slowly from offsets; best established from corms in fall.
